data.title: ABN AMRO: Asset Quality Continues To Support Earnings
data.description:
Driven by ongoing low credit charges, Dutch lender ABN AMRO (OTCPK:AAVMY) (OTCPK:ABMRF) delivered a modest bottom-line beat for the first quarter, with attributable net income of €568 million landing ahead of company-compiled consensus. Provision-fueled beats often don't win much kudos from the market, but with a stronger-than-expected capital ratio strengthening the case for buybacks, ABN is currently up around 7% on its pre-results closing price, lifting it to its highest level since 2019. As for the ADSs, they've received an extra boost from a weaker U.S. dollar. That's helped drive an exceptional 57% return so far in 2025, including a roughly 33% return since my last update in February.

Back then, ABN was trading for just ~0.65x tangible book value ('TBV') despite earning a ~10% return on tangible equity. This pointed to a price/earnings multiple of less than 7x and, based on its usual 50% payout ratio, a high single-digit dividend yield. Fresh results provide a nice opportunity to revisit ABN's value case. Although its shares have re-rated, I remain bullish.

Asset Quality Still Padding Results

ABN AMRO has started its fiscal 2025 in seemingly good form, with first quarter attributable net income of €568 million (€0.69 per share) beating consensus by around €30 million, or 6%. This mapped to an annualized return on equity of 9.9% (similar based on tangible equity), in line with management's 9%-10% medium-term goal, putting ABN on course to meet initial 2025 consensus EPS of €2.39.

While the bottom-line beat was driven by lower than expected credit charges (more on that below), ABN's pre-provision performance looked reasonable enough, and management kept full-year guidance for the main drivers (net interest income and operating costs) in place.

Starting with revenue, net interest income accounts for over 70% of ABN's top line. Although it is a relatively simple bank, this line can still be somewhat complicated to get a handle on, with dynamics like its deposit hedges, mix shift, asset margins, volumes et al. all playing a role.

Broadly speaking, the first quarter saw weaker margins offset by better-than-expected volumes. Starting with the former, ABN's net interest margin fell 8bps year-on-year to 1.54%, missing consensus by the same amount. Lower interest rates are weakening deposit margins (the spread between swap rates and deposit costs) while asset margins are also a headwind, specifically in mortgages. Offsetting this, both loans and deposits grew year-on-year and came in higher than expected. All said, ABN generated €1.56 billion in first quarter NII, flat year-on-year and virtually in line with consensus.

Fee income was a little stronger, rising 8% year-on-year to €507 million and beating by around 2%. In its retail business, a pick-up in the Dutch mortgage market was good for higher advisory fees, while its corporate wing saw fees rise due to higher transaction volumes in its clearing business. ABN should close on its purchase of fee-heavy German private bank Hauck Aufhäuser Lampe in Q2, bolstering total fee income in the remaining quarters.

Turning to costs, operating costs were €1.31 billion. This was virtually in line with consensus, leaving the bank on track to meet guidance of €5.3-€5.4 billion for the full year. As for credit, I thought this could be a source of upside last time, and this looks like it is playing out, with ABN booking just €5 million in credit costs last quarter. This beat by over €60 million and equated to a cost of risk of less than 1bp. Higher residential real estate prices are credit positive for ABN, and with Dutch house prices up over 10% year-on-year, lower loan-to-value ratios ('LTV') contributed to a release in its retail business, offsetting higher charges on the corporate side.

Clearly, this line is padding the bank's profitability right now. If credit charges had landed in line with management's through-the-cycle target (15-20bps), ABN's annualized ROE would have been around 140bps lower. While that is something to bear in mind going forward, I would note that asset quality remains excellent, with various metrics (Stage 2 and 3 Ratios, percentage of residential mortgages in arrears, Forbearance Ratio, loans past due, etc.) either stable or improving quarter-on-quarter. This can help keep credit costs contained in the near term.

For readers worried about the possible impact of tariffs, I would think that ABN is probably one of the least exposed European banks to trade disruption, given over 60% of its loans are in Dutch residential mortgages.

Per management, losses on mortgages are mainly due to a combination of rising unemployment (which leads to an increase in defaults) and falling house prices (resulting in negative equity and a loss on disposal). In terms of the former, unemployment in the Netherlands remains near its recent historical lows, as shown in the chart above. On the latter, ABN's weighted average LTV is around 53%, with only ~2% of mortgages above 100%, as shown below.

Capital Surprises To The Upside

While credit-driven beats are often considered lower quality by the market, ABN shares are currently around 7% higher than their pre-results closing price. One possible explanation for that is the strength of ABN's balance sheet, with the bank reporting a Common Equity Tier 1 capital ratio of 14.7% at the end of last quarter, 50bps above consensus.

Why is a beat here noteworthy? Well, having historically delivered buyback updates at the beginning of the year, ABN delayed this year's plan to post-2Q25 results, disappointing the market. I did think this was a timing issue and nothing else, but anyway, a beat on capital levels bolsters the case for buybacks when it does finalize its offering. That's because management targets a 13.5% CET1, which implies around €1.7 billion in surplus capital - equal to roughly 10% of its current market value. Since ABN is primarily a yield stock (dividends and buybacks will drive most of its returns from here), the market's positive reaction to results makes sense.

Shares Still Look Reasonably Valued

ABN shares trade for €21.17 as I type ($23.67 per ADS), up around 25% from my last update (the ADSs have done better due to the weaker dollar). Naturally, that makes the stock a little less cheap than it was; relative to prior-quarter TBV, ABN has re-rated from 0.63x to 0.77x, while its price/earnings multiple has expanded from 7.1x to 8.5x (based on 2025 consensus EPS).

I think the shares remain reasonably valued in spite of this. In terms of earnings, consensus hasn't moved all that much. Analysts still expect ABN to make around €2 billion in annual attributable net income between 2025 and 2027, and this would keep the forward P/E in the single-digit area.

In turn, the above should map to a pretty attractive shareholder yield. Since ABN's organic growth potential is limited, it needs to maintain a relatively high total payout ratio in order to avoid capital accumulation. ABN's dividend is the main tool for achieving this, with management aiming to pay out 50% of net income this way. This implies a forward yield of ~6%. I think ABN could manage a further €500 million per annum in buybacks out of earnings, and that would take its total shareholder yield to around 9%. Given ABN's stronger-than-expected CET1, there's scope for additional capital returns as well, pushing the total yield over 10%.

More broadly, I think a mature bank earning a 9%-10% ROE should really trade for around 0.9x-1.0x book value, and this would still leave ABN undervalued by roughly 20%-30%.

Conclusion

ABN AMRO is off to a sound start to 2025, with first quarter earnings topping sell-side expectations. While provision-fueled beats don't usually elicit the kind of positive response that ABN saw post-results, a stronger than expected regulatory capital ratio augurs well for next quarter's update on buybacks. As for the investment case, while these shares have re-rated since my last update, a single-digit multiple of forward earnings continues to look cheap. Accordingly, I remain bullish.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Arlo: Executing Well Against Long-Term Expansion Plans
data.description:
With stock markets roaring back to all-time highs, I continue to focus my single-stock investing on small-cap value names that have a larger valuation disconnect to the broader markets. Many of these stocks have already outperformed large-cap peers this year precisely because their valuations were undemanding: and in my view, that outperformance is set to continue.

Arlo (NYSE:ARLO) is a fantastic small-cap play. The home security camera vendor has continued to sign on hundreds of thousands of new paid subscribers, validating and exceeding the company's ambitious long-term goals. Amid a sharp rise in adjusted EBITDA profitability, to me Arlo's already-healthy ~25% rally since the start of the year is the beginning of a longer win streak.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Arlo in March, when the stock was just rallying to ~$13 on the back of its Q4 earnings print. To me, the company's recent Q1 earnings release and its robust subscriber adds have given investors even more to cheer: and seeing as Arlo has barely just recovered its post-tariff losses, investors still have a chance to enter into this stock at a modest price. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

It's prudent to zoom out and consider this company's goals for the long term. By 2030, Arlo wants to hit 10 million paid subscribers (roughly double where it ended its most recent quarter), while also roughly doubling adjusted operating margins to ~25% as well.

Arlo has an excellent track record for hitting its growth targets early. It hit 4.9 million paid subscribers in its most recent quarter, noted on its earnings call that it broke the 5 million mark early in Q2: a year and a half earlier than its targets. This is a healthy indicator that Arlo is effectively growing its business via its strategy of selling cheaper hardware to get customers in the door, while still keeping a close eye on margins and ensuring long-term scaling.

In spite of Arlo's current momentum, the stock remains reasonably valued. At current share prices near $14, Arlo trades at a market cap of $1.41 billion. After we net off the $165.6 million of cash on the company's most recent balance sheet, Arlo's resulting enterprise value is $1.24 billion.

Meanwhile, for the full year FY25, Arlo has maintained its full-year revenue outlook of $510-$540 million, or flat to 6% y/y growth.

In Q1, Arlo had improved adjusted EBITDA margins at a 630bps y/y pace (to 13.8%). If we assume this pace of margin expansion holds throughout FY25, FY25 adjusted EBITDA margins would be 14.2%, leading to an adjusted EBITDA of $74.3 million (+84% y/y) at the midpoint. This positions Arlo's valuation at 16.5x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA.

For a company that is still nearly doubling adjusted EBITDA, and is expecting a ~doubling of its subscriber and ARR base through the next five years, I think Arlo still has plenty of room to grow into its valuation.

Stay long here and keep riding Arlo's post-earnings momentum higher.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Arlo's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Arlo's revenue declined -4% y/y to $68.8 million, driven by an expected -26% y/y decline in hardware (which is due partially to the company dropping its pricing) while seeing a much stronger 21% y/y growth in subscription revenue, which now represents nearly 60% of total revenue. We note that Arlo's total revenue beat Wall Street's expectations of $118.4 million (-5% y/y).

What investors truly cheered, however, was the company's aggressive new paid user adds. The company added 299k new paid accounts in Q1, which was almost on par with 364k net adds in Q4 (which tends to be the biggest quarter by far due to year-end holiday shopping around Black Friday and Christmas). Meanwhile, the company added $19 million of net-new ARR to end the quarter at $276.4 million (+22% y/y), which was more than the $16 million of ARR that Arlo added sequentially in Q4.

Management notes that the company is planning a huge product launch in year-end 2025 that can drive new hardware sales (and new paid account growth). Importantly, the company notes that this new generation of products has a 20-35% lower bill of materials, which can substantially help profitability in the hardware segment and reduce overall subscriber acquisition costs. Per CEO Matt McRae's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Given our track-record of success, disciplined execution and strategic agility, I believe the current conditions and volatility favors Arlo and may present the company with new opportunities for growth. As a reminder, Arlo is planning its largest product launch in company history for the 2025 holiday season with over 100 new SKUs launching into our channel. These new products will substantially extend our technology differentiation and achieve a 20% to 35% cost reduction, which puts us in a great position to not only mitigate any tariff impact but also win incremental opportunities to capture share in Q4 and 2026."

Here's one risk we have to keep in mind, however. Retail and distribution channel inventories grew in the March quarter, hitting ~13 weeks of supply in the U.S. and ~8 weeks of supply in Asia Pacific. This means that U.S. distributors have enough units on hand to last an entire quarter without restocking. This was higher than ~12 weeks of supply in the U.S. and ~6 weeks in Asia in the year-ago Q1.

This may signal that Arlo's hardware revenue could decelerate further ahead of its product launch if partner replenishment orders are low.

On the profitability front, we note that Arlo's gross margins rose 620bps y/y to 44.3%, despite a negative products gross margin - driven by the favorable mix shift in revenue toward subscriptions, and a validation of the company's customer acquisition strategy to pull in new accounts by offering attractive hardware sales.

Adjusted EBITDA, meanwhile, jumped 630bps y/y as well to 13.8%. The company notes that baseline tariffs of 10% will have a minor impact to gross margins going forward (less than 25% of total revenue comes from hardware imported to the U.S.), and it plans to absorb the cost with no consumer-facing price increases.

Key takeaways

Arlo remains a solid long-term hold in the small-cap space as the security camera company gears up toward a major product launch later this year that has the potential to materially lift its gross margin profile. The company has hit its ambitious subscriber growth targets for 2027 early, giving us plenty of confidence in the company's plan to ~double subscribers further by 2030.

Stay long here and hold out for a bigger rally.

data.title: April's Shockwave: Tariff Drama, Dollar Drift And The Small-Cap Squeeze
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

April 2025 might be remembered not just for the policies announced, but for the ripple effects they unleashed across asset classes. It started with a bang — "Liberation Day,"1 as some called it — a declaration of sweeping tariffs unlike anything seen in years. Market participants didn't just react. They recalibrated. The exchange-traded fund/exchange-traded products (ETF/ETPs) scorecard from WisdomTree tells a compelling tale of that recalibration.

The Pulse of April: Global Hedges and Anti-Dollar Bets

The month of April was about clarity. Not in the traditional sense — markets were volatile and uncertain — but clarity in narrative. Investors voted with capital: they favored hard assets, international exposure and anything that got them away from U.S. dollar exposure.

Figure 1 indicates the top five and bottom five WisdomTree ETF/ETPs, ranked by total return performance, for the month of April 2025.

Select Comments on the Top Five ETF/ETPs for April 2025

The WisdomTree Bitcoin Fund (BTCW)2 returned 13.25%, topping the list of best-performing ETF/ETPs at WisdomTree during April 2025. In a world where fiat risk was back on the table, Bitcoin served as the opt-out clause. The WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Gold Miners Strategy Fund (GDMN),3 a strategy with exposure to both gold and gold miners, returned 11.18% as investors sought real assets. Both the WisdomTree Europe SmallCap Dividend Fund (DFE)4 at 7.12% and the WisdomTree International MidCap Dividend Fund (DIM)5 at 6.21% showed a preference for non-U.S. equities. We also note that that smaller companies typically do less exporting, so it's possible investors are looking abroad and looking for companies not selling their products as much abroad, for instance, back to the U.S.

Select Comments on the Bottom Five ETF/ETPs for April 2025

Meanwhile, U.S. small-cap strategies — WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Fund (EES),6 WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Dividend Fund (DES),7 WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Dividend Growth Fund (DGRS)8 — sank. The companies in these Funds, often domestically focused and rate-sensitive, now face rising input costs, tariff-induced distortions and constrained pricing power. The market appears to be saying: "Until we understand how Main Street U.S. digests this, we're stepping back." Small-cap stocks can also be among the most sensitive to a recession, should one materialize, which we know is a topic of ongoing debate.

Figure 1: WisdomTree's Top and Bottom Five ETF/ETPs by Performance in April 2025

A Wider Lens: Four Months That Define a Regime

Figure 2 zooms out to year-to-date returns, where there is a broad similarity to some of the aspects we saw in figure 1. GDMN dominated the first four months with a 68.43% return. We know that many investors have been watching the price of gold hit new highs, even crossing the $3,500 per troy ounce level for the first time. GDMN builds in an exposure to gold and an exposure to gold miners, companies that may actually make more money as the price of gold rises.

The WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Equity Strategy Fund (GDE)9 captures the same overall theme that we mentioned for, just instead of the equity component being gold miners it is a broad basket of U.S. large-cap stocks. Other top performers? WisdomTree International AI Enhanced Value Fund (AIVI),10 WisdomTree International High Dividend Fund (DTH)11 and WisdomTree International LargeCap Dividend Fund (DOL),12 which are all equity strategies focused on the developed international space. If April was a tactical pivot, year-to-date returns suggest a foundational repricing of what matters. Investors are favoring assets that produce cash, sell real stuff and live outside the U.S. dollar's shadow.

Bottom of the list? The WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Growth Fund (QSML),13 DGRS, EES, the WisdomTree Cloud Computing Fund (WCLD)14 and DES. They represent what markets are currently discounting: the sensitivity of smaller market cap companies to a potential economic slowdown, and a move away from more speculative, higher-multiple tech.

Figure 2: WisdomTree's Top and Bottom 5 ETF/ETPs by Performance Year-to-Date 2025

What the Data Hints At: Beyond the Obvious

When we juxtapose April and YTD, a few deeper themes emerge:

  • The U.S. dollar has been depreciating. Many of the top performers get a performance boost when the value of the U.S. dollar drops. It's clear that market participants are trying to digest what the moves from the Trump Administration mean in both the shorter and longer term for the U.S. dollar.
  • Small caps can be very sensitive to the economic cycle. If there is an economic slowdown or a recession as a result of these tariff policies, it's clear that the equities taking the brunt of this risk may be found within the U.S. small-cap segment of the market.

To Go with the Recent Strength or to Move Toward the Performance Weakness

There is little question that GDMN has had a remarkable four-month run, and investors may get a bit skittish on seeing that result. GDE, with its equity component focused on 500 large-cap U.S. stocks, may be the more conservative option for investors thinking about the nexus of gold and U.S. equities as a strategy.

On the other hand, we think it's possible that the tariff policy is so fluid and so tough to analyze that there is a chance the negative performance of U.S. small caps could be overdone. Investors with dry powder might start looking in that direction, as there can be different announcements with positive impacts to the market that could benefit that more beaten-down segment.

In any event, we look forward to continuing to analyze what the top and bottom-performing ETF/ETPs at WisdomTree will tell us over the coming periods.

Footnotes

DOL: Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development related to such sectors. This may result in greater share price volatility. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time.

DIM: Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors and/or smaller companies increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development related to such sectors or companies. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time. This may result in greater share price volatility.

DFE: Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors and/or smaller companies increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development related to such sectors or companies. This may result in greater share price volatility.

GDE: The Fund is actively managed and invests in U.S.-listed gold futures and U.S. equity securities. The Fund’s use of U.S.-listed gold futures contracts will give rise to leverage, magnifying gains and losses and causing the Fund to be more volatile than if it had not been leveraged. Moreover, the price movements in gold and gold futures contracts may fluctuate quickly and dramatically, and have a historically low correlation with the returns of the stock and bond markets. U.S. equity securities, such as common stocks, are subject to market, economic and business risks that may cause their prices to fluctuate. The Fund’s investment strategy will also require it to redeem shares for cash or to otherwise include cash as part of its redemption proceeds, which may cause the Fund to recognize capital gains.

WCLD: The Fund invests in cloud computing companies, which are heavily dependent on the internet and utilizing a distributed network of servers over the internet. Cloud computing companies may have limited product lines, markets, financial resources or personnel and are subject to the risks of changes in business cycles, world economic growth, technological progress, and government regulation. These companies typically face intense competition and potentially rapid product obsolescence. Additionally, many cloud computing companies store sensitive consumer information and could be the target of cybersecurity attacks and other types of theft, which could have a negative impact on these companies and the Fund. Securities of cloud computing companies tend to be more volatile than securities of companies that rely less heavily on technology and, specifically, on the internet. Cloud computing companies can typically engage in significant amounts of spending on research and development, and rapid changes to the field could have a material adverse effect on a company’s operating results. The composition of the Index is heavily dependent on quantitative and qualitative information and data from one or more third parties, and the Index may not perform as intended.

QSML: Growth stocks, as a group, may be out of favor with the market and underperform value stocks or the overall equity market. Growth stocks are generally more sensitive to market movements than other types of stocks. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors and/or smaller companies increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development related to such sectors or companies. This may result in greater share price volatility. The Fund is non-diversified, as a result, changes in the market value of a single security could cause greater fluctuations in the value of Fund shares than would occur in a diversified fund. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit. The Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets and the Index may not perform as intended. The securities of small-capitalization companies generally trade in lower volumes and are subject to greater and more unpredictable price changes than larger-capitalization stocks or the stock market as a whole. Small-capitalization companies may be particularly sensitive to adverse economic developments as well as changes in interest rates, government regulation, borrowing costs and earnings. The Fund is new and has limited operating history.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

data.title: Independent Bank: Operating Environment Signals A Slowdown
data.description:
Earnings of Independent Bank Corporation (NASDAQ:IBCP) will likely suffer from anticipated margin compression and a slowdown in loan growth. I’m expecting earnings to dip by 5% to $3.00 per share in 2025. Compared to my last report on the company, I’ve reduced my earnings estimate. My updated target price is below the current market price. Based on a total expected return of negative 6.6%, I’m maintaining a hold rating on Independent Bank Corporation.

Michigan’s Economic Condition Suggests Loan Growth Could Remain Low

Independent Bank’s loan growth slowed down to 0.8% in the first quarter of 2025 from 2.4% in the last quarter of 2024. On an annualized basis, the loan growth in the first quarter was a disappointing 3.3%, which is below last year’s growth of 6.5%, and the last five-year compounded annual growth rate of 8.2%.

The management believes that if Michigan’s economy remains stable, then it can achieve 5%-6% loan growth this year, mostly on the back of commercial loans, as mentioned in the earnings presentation. I think the management will miss its target, firstly because of the first quarter’s performance, and secondly because of the operating environment.

As shown below, IBCP’s commercial segment, which makes up around half of total loans, is well diversified by loan type. Manufacturing, retail, industrial real estate, and healthcare are the biggest classes within the commercial segment.

The tariffs were intended to promote manufacturing in the US, but the US Government’s eagerness to strike trade deals shows that the government itself doesn’t believe that manufacturing can shift from other countries to the US. The latest reading for the Purchasing Managers Index (“PMI”) released in May shows that the manufacturing industry is still contracting (below 50) even after the tariff announcement.

The auto industry of Detroit, MI, is interlinked with parts suppliers in Canada and Mexico; therefore, the auto manufacturers were concerned when tariffs were first announced. The tariffs have been eased for now, as per news sources. However, they haven’t been completely eliminated. The financial blow to the manufacturers from the tariffs could reduce the equity they could put forth for capital expenditure. Banks may not want to lend if the borrowers are cash-strapped and hurting financially.

Furthermore, there is a possibility that the tariffs could be reversed by the current government or the next government. Backward integration is costly, and auto manufacturers might not want to risk making a hefty investment and then seeing it all go to waste if tariffs are reversed. The resultant indecisiveness and wait-and-see approach could hurt credit offtake in this industry.

As for retail, IBCP’s second big exposure, I believe supply shocks would discourage borrowers from expanding their retail businesses. Container bookings are down by around 60% because of the tariffs, and the Fed’s Chairman commented earlier this week that “We may be entering a period of more frequent, and potentially more persistent, supply shocks,” as per news reports.

Further, Michigan’s coincident economic index suggests that economic activity continued to fall in April.

Considering these factors, I’m expecting the loan book to grow by 3.9% in 2025, which is below both the management’s guidance and my previous expectation.

Margin to Dip Slightly Due to Rate Cuts

In contrast to my previous expectations, the net interest margin continued to inch upward over the last two quarters. I was expecting the margin to dip slightly because of interest rate cuts. However, the deposit cost ended up declining more than the asset yields, which widened the margin.

Both the asset yields and deposit costs are quite sensitive to interest rate changes, as discussed below.

  1. As can be deciphered from the details given in the presentation, around half of the loans (excluding installment loans) were fixed and half were variable at the end of March 2025.
  2. Savings and interest-bearing demand deposits made up 44% of total deposits at the end of March 2025. These deposits normally re-price down soon after a rate cut.

I’m expecting the Fed to cut its Fed funds rate by 50 basis points in the second half of this year. The management’s rate-sensitivity model showed that a 100-basis-point rate cut could reduce the net interest income by just 0.63%.

Considering these factors, I’m expecting the net interest margin to dip by 4 basis points in the last three quarters of 2025.

Expecting the EPS to Dip by 5% to $3.00

The management is expecting non-interest income to drop this year by 14%-14.5%, as mentioned in the earnings presentation. It was already down by a hefty 45.5% in 1Q-2025 from 4Q-2024 because of the Mortgage Loan Servicing sub-head. I’ve decided to take the management’s guidance for the non-interest income. This line item will most probably drag earnings this year.

On the other hand, the low loan growth will likely drive earnings. Meanwhile, the margin’s anticipated change will barely affect the bottom line. To forecast the non-interest expense, I’m using the management’s guidance in the earnings presentation of mid-single-digit growth.

I think the provision expense will remain rangebound near the level seen over the last three years. I don’t think the trade war could push up the provision expense too much because the company has already built up excessive reserves for loan losses. The ratio of allowance for credit losses to non-performing losses was at an amazing 989.32% at the end of March 2025, as mentioned in the earnings release.

Overall, I’m expecting the company to report earnings of $3.00 per share for 2025, down 5% from last year. In my last report, I projected an EPS of $3.04 for 2025. My new EPS estimate is lower because of the management’s guidance on non-interest income and non-interest expense. Further, my loan growth estimate is lower now.

Maintaining a Hold Rating

When I last wrote about Independent Bank in October 2024, I adopted a hold rating. The stock price has dipped by just 0.64% since then, in line with expectations. I need to update my valuation now, mostly because the trade war has significantly changed the company’s operating environment.

Historically, Independent Bank has traded at an average price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) ratio of 1.497x and a price-to-earnings (“P/E”) multiple of 8.039x.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with the forecast earnings per share of $3.00 gives a target price of $24.1 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with the forecast tangible book value per share of $23.6 gives a target price of $35.3 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $29.7, which is 9.4% below the May 15 closing stock price. Independent Bank also offers a dividend yield of 3.2% at a quarterly dividend per share of $0.26. Taking the sum of the price downside and the dividend yield gives a total expected return of negative 6.6%. This isn’t bad enough for a sell rating. Hence, I’m maintaining a hold rating on Independent Bank Corporation.

data.title: DVLU: High Concentration Risks For This Dorsey Wright Momentum And Value ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The First Trust Dorsey Wright Momentum & Value ETF (NASDAQ:DVLU) is comprised of 50 large/mid-cap value stocks that pass a proprietary intermediate and long-term momentum screen and also score well on up to four traditional valuation ratios. However, the selection process compares stocks against a broad market benchmark, not their sector peers, and with no sector constraints in place, the result is a portfolio with 55% allocated to the Financials sector. As a result, concentration risk is substantial, and performance is inconsistent, which I will demonstrate in the article below and used to help justify my "sell" rating. I hope you enjoy the read.

DVLU Overview

Strategy Discussion

DVLU tracks the Dorsey Wright Momentum Plus Value Index, which selects stocks from the NASDAQ US Large Mid Cap Index that pass a proprietary momentum screening process. Afterward, eligible securities receive a value score based on four value metrics:

  • Price to Sales
  • Price to Book
  • Price to Earnings
  • Price to Cash Flow

Securities are not penalized if one of these ratios is not available. However, they must have at least three ratios available to receive a value score. Lastly, the 50 securities with the highest value scores are selected for the Index, and securities are weighted based on the strength of their factor score. There are no sector allocation caps, and the Index reconstitutes quarterly following the third Friday in January, April, July, and October.

The Dorsey Wright Momentum Approach

The Dorsey Wright approach to momentum investing relies on Point & Figure Charting, which, according to Nasdaq, "uses end-of-day pricing to capture significant price movements" and assigns a security's price movements to one of 11 different chart patterns, which are all just variations of the Double Top and Double Bottom patterns.

Nasdaq analysts backtested the strategy from 1990-2023 and assigned one of four groups to each of the 1,000 largest U.S. securities by market cap: BX, BO, SX, and SO, representing the "Best," "Good," "Improving," and "Very Weak" groups. At first glance, the results below are incredibly convincing. The equal-weighted "Best" group produced a cumulative 7,973% total return compared to 3,354% and 2,622% for the selection universe and the S&P 500 Total Return Index, respectively.

The "Best" portfolio also produced an annual return of 13.78% compared to 10.20% for the S&P 500 Index, but that's actually the annualized return over 34 years, which is impacted by timing. It's equivalent to using a linear-scaled chart rather than a log-scaled chart, which is not standard practice. One possible reason is that had the analysts highlighted the following median annual returns, the data would have been less convincing, as follows:

  • BXw: 14.99%
  • BO: 14.99%
  • SX: 10.61%
  • SO: 12.75%
  • Universe: 15.09%
  • SPXTR: 14.38%

This method assigns less importance to outlier years like 1999 when the "Best" portfolio gained 68.41% compared to 21.04% for the S&P 500. It also shows that the median return for the selection universe was actually stronger than the "Best" and "Good" groups (15.09% vs. 14.99%), and the S&P 500 wasn't too far behind at 14.38%, so the typical returns aren't that much, if any, of an improvement. Investors must take on extra risk and significantly outperform in a few years to be successful, and that hasn't happened enough yet. As shown below, the Invesco Dorsey Wright Momentum ETF (PDP) has trailed SPY by 1.34% per year since March 2007, and two of the biggest outperforming years (2007, 2020) were quickly offset with deeper losses in the subsequent years.

Peer Group Performance Summary

In this article, I will compare DVLU's performance and fundamentals with four ETFs, as follows:

  1. iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD)
  2. First Trust Dorsey Wright Momentum & Dividend ETF (DDIV)
  3. VictoryShares US Value Momentum ETF (ULVM)
  4. WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund ETF (WTV)

First Trust's DVLU and DDIV have the highest expense ratios at 0.60%, ULVM, another fund that aims to select stocks with strong momentum and value features, charges only 0.20% per year. I selected IWD as proxy for DVLU's selection universe, and WTV because I know it to be a solid all-around fund with strong momentum characteristics at the moment.

Since DVLU launched on September 5, 2018, it has delivered a 62.87% total return compared to 79.20% for DDIV, indicating that the dividend screens, as applied to the same Dorsey Wright momentum method, was more effective than the value screens. IWD and ULVM also outperformed by 10-11%, while WTV led with a 118.79% gain.

Furthermore, the following analysis using Portfolio Visualizer's asset backtest tool reveals that DVLU's volatility, as measured by annualized standard deviation, was much higher than its peers at 25.50%. This is consistent with the earlier backtest for the momentum strategy alone, but judging by how much more volatile it was compared to IWD, incorporating the value screens added even more risk. Investors might prefer gaining access to the value factor through DDIV's dividend approach, as that resulted in less total risk and only a marginally lower "Best Year" result (39.92% vs. 41.64%).

DVLU Analysis

Sector Allocations vs. Peer Group

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between DVLU, IWD, DDIV, ULVM, and WTV. It's here where we see the impact of having no sector constraints, as DVLU allocates 55.38% to Financials, including 10.75% to Regional Banks and 20.84% to various insurance sub-industries.

ULVM is much more balanced across all sectors and only has 22.08% allocated to Financials, and since it also screens for value and momentum, it could be a nice option. Meanwhile, WTV achieves its strong value features by screening for shareholder yield (dividends and buybacks), and instead of momentum, it looks for high-quality stocks.

DVLU Fundamental Analysis: Top 25 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for DVLU's top 25 holdings, which comprise 67.69% of the portfolio. DDIV is just as concentrated at 70.77%, while IWD, ULVM, and WTV are much better diversified choices.

Here are two observations to consider:

1. DVLU's value features appear strong. We see that it trades at 12.96x forward earnings and 1.91x trailing book value, which are the cheapest among the peer group. However, we must also acknowledge that these ratios are low primarily because it overweights the Financials sector, which is traditionally cheap. I adjusted for that by calculating value scores from Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, which compares stocks against their sector peers, and DVLU's 4.00/10 score does not stand out. In fact, DDIV scored better at 4.08/10 despite being "dividend" and not "value" focused, which some might consider to be a distinction without a difference. ULVM and WTV also look strong at 4.05/10 and 3.87/10, respectively. Therefore, I conclude that DVLU is a strong value fund if you're sector agnostic, but its components don't stand out when compared to their sector peers.

2. On the other hand, DVLU's momentum features do stand out, both on an absolute and sector-adjusted basis. Using more traditional metrics as opposed to the Dorsey Wright Point & Figure approach, we see that DVLU's components have gained 27.17% over the last year on a weighted average basis, which is more than twice the Russell 1000 Value Index benchmark. Furthermore, its 7.24/10 sector-adjusted momentum score is meaningfully better than IWD, and a likely reason is its excellent earnings growth potential, estimated at 14.13% over the next twelve months.

Investment Recommendation

I'm not confident recommending DVLU to readers because it appears designed to be non-diversified at the sector level, thereby increasing risks and leading to inconsistent and possibly poor long-term returns. While DVLU currently has strong momentum features, its components' value characteristics aren't better when compared to their sector peers, and I'm also not convinced that the Dorsey Wright approach to momentum investing has any benefit, given the poor active returns of the Invesco Dorsey Wright Momentum ETF over the last 18 years. Therefore, I've decided to assign a "sell" rating to DVLU. Thank you for reading.

data.title: UPGD: Portfolio Of Analysts' Darlings Investors Should Be Realistic About
data.description:
While there is a myriad of rules for successful investing, I believe that being realistic is perhaps what matters most. And the Invesco Bloomberg Analyst Rating Improvers ETF (NYSEARCA:UPGD) is one example that illustrates that quite clearly.

I initiated coverage of this ETF in 2023 when it was known as the Invesco Raymond James SB-1 Equity ETF (traded with a ticker RYJ), with a neutral rating. And in 2024, when it saw a deep strategy change and was renamed, I did not become bullish either. Why? Because the idea that lies at the crux of it might be convincing, but returns are not.

In fairness, the fact that analysts have become more bullish on a stock does not postulate that they are 1) perfectly right or 2) this stock is poised to outperform the market.

And today, I am not decidedly bullish on it. While I should admit that assuming the market is gradually moving from fear to greed amid the easing of the tariff drama, and UPGD might deliver some gains mirroring the movement of the broad market in the near term (unless there are macro shocks), I see no argument why it should be bought into, as it has a few significant vulnerabilities.

The issues include its triple-digit turnover, a high fee, and unimpressive returns, as it captures too much of the market's downside and too little of its upside. These are intricate issues, so I will give them the necessary amount of attention below in the note.

UPGD Strategy Recap

But first, what is the essence of UPGD's strategy?

I would like to remind my dear readers that even though the fund's history traces back to 2006, we have to ignore almost 18 years of its existence and returns delivered over this period, as in March 2024, it made a sharp turn and changed its strategy, abandoning the Raymond James SB-1 Equity Index and opting for the Bloomberg ANR Improvers Index.

As we know from the Invesco website, this index

... aims to select the 50 companies with the highest analyst recommendation improvers score from the Bloomberg US Large Mid Universe over the prior 6- and 12- month periods based on Bloomberg Analyst Recommendations. The Index and Fund are rebalanced quarterly in March, June, September, and December.

The management fee was also trimmed from 75 bps to 40 bps, boosting the fund's appeal to cost-conscious investors. In my view, it is still rather high, and if maintained at the same level, it will significantly detract from total returns over the long term.

What Returns UPGD Has Been Capable of Delivering?

While the strategy is seemingly promising a lot as it is focused on analysts' darlings, UPGD's returns have been far from excellent, though with nuances.

First, since my skeptical February 2025 note, UPGD has outperformed the S&P 500 index, which is a nice surprise.

But this is just the tip of the iceberg. The most interesting story comes to light when we are scrutinizing its performance since the strategy change, juxtaposed with that of the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV):

This is not a complete disaster, but this is clearly not something I would be satisfied seeing from a high-turnover fund chasing stocks that won rating upgrades from analysts.

Looking at the annualized returns and a few selected risk metrics delivered over April 2024–April 2025, the story becomes even more interesting as UPGD not only had a lower return but also a higher standard deviation than IVV and also a deeper maximum drawdown.

What is especially worrisome is that UPGD was delivering weaker results both when the market was going higher (this point is supported by the upside capture ratio) and when it was going lower (the downside capture ratio illustrates that vividly).

How Has The UPGD Portfolio Changed Since February 2025?

Composition

UPGD has a high-turnover strategy, so investors should be prepared for large cohorts of stocks being added and removed during every quarterly rebalancing.

For example, as of May 13, UPGD had 50 stocks in its portfolio, with just 46.8% of the net assets allocated to stocks that were present in the February version. I compiled the key deletions in the table below:

Stock Weight as of February 5, 2025
Henry Schein, Inc. (HSIC) 2.245%
eBay Inc. (EBAY) 2.240%
EPAM Systems, Inc. (EPAM) 2.218%

Created using data from UPGD

And the largest additions were as follows:

Stock Weight as of May 13, 2025
Okta, Inc. (OKTA) 2.628%
Northrop Grumman Corporation (NOC) 2.231%
Consolidated Edison, Inc. (ED) 2.151%

Created using data from UPGD

Sectors

I would not say that UPGD has made something of a U-turn regarding its sector preferences, yet the mix has clearly changed.

For example, while its allocation to defensive stocks is still significant, the consumer staples sector, which was its main allocation in September 2024 and February 2025, is no longer in the top position. At the same time, industrials has gained substantially, now being its most important sector. Besides, financials were added to the mix (thanks to PayPal (PYPL)), while real estate is still ignored.

Seeking Alpha Quant, Wall Street, Seeking Alpha Analysts Scores

In my previous note, I emphasized that despite UPGD's strategy having "Bloomberg Analyst Recommendations" at its core, IVV still has higher weighted average Wall Street Analysts, Seeking Alpha Quant, and Seeking Alpha Analysts ratings. And this point is still valid.

Score UPGD (May 2025) UPGD (February 2025) UPGD (September 2024) IVV (May 2025)
Quant Rating 3.18 3.25 3.32 3.38
Seeking Alpha Analysts 3.54 3.39 3.35 3.57
Wall St. Analysts 3.58 3.61 3.51 4.09

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Factors

Deep changes in a portfolio's composition may or may not result in shifts in factor exposures. For example, value stocks can be replaced with value stocks, mega-caps with mega-caps, essentially resulting in a zero or near-zero impact on a basket's factor profile. But in the case of UPGD, this time, we see a few rather significant shifts.

To make it easier for my readers to understand whether UPGD now has a value, growth, or quality tilt compared to the market (namely the S&P 500), I added a few selected metrics for IVV.

Size and value factors

  • UPGD's weighted average market cap has nearly doubled, touching the highest level since September 2024. However, this fact is mostly the consequence of just one factor—the addition of Tesla (TSLA), which now has a 1.9% weight in this portfolio.
  • The adjusted weighted average earnings yield (loss-making companies were removed), though higher than that of IVV, is at the lowest level since September.
  • EV/EBITDA has spiked, but again owing to just one factor—OKTA has that ratio at 802.25x. Adjusted for its impact, the WA ratio goes down to 17.2x, which is more in line with where it was in the past.
  • However, what is more important is that the share of stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade or stronger has fallen to the lowest level since September.

Quality

The additions have definitely bolstered UPGD's quality characteristics, which is evident from the higher weighted average net income margin, adjusted Return on Equity, and Return on Assets. And the share of stocks with a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher is on par with that of IVV.

Growth

A few significant improvements on that front are worth touching upon. For example, its weighted average forward EPS growth rate has risen remarkably, mostly because fewer companies in its portfolio are now forecast to deliver EPS contraction going forward. And the removal of HF Sinclair Corporation (DINO), which had a 44.9% forward EPS growth rate as of February, clearly helped.

Nevertheless, all UPGD's metrics are much weaker than those of IVV, so it clearly does not have a growth tilt.

Final Thoughts

In summary, it is worth being realistic about UPGD. Analysts' darlings can beat the market over certain periods, yet consistency is something I do not expect.

There are other concerns. As asset flows have been rather weak, UPGD has only $107.4 million in AUM. I should remind here that the lower the AUM, the higher the risk of a fund's liquidation.

Overall, UPGD's liquidity leaves a lot to be desired. In fact, UPGD is D-rated on that parameter by the Quant system.

And the median bid/ask spread of 12 bps (as of May 15, as per the UPGD website) does not look comfortable. So it should be rather complicated to establish a sizable position for a desirable price.

So considering all of the above, while I was diligently trying to find a reason for an upgrade, there will be none, so UPGD still belongs in the cohort of those ETFs with a conservative Hold rating.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Unrealized Depreciation Nearly Universal In Q1
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up—highlighting individual news and events—as well as the top-down—providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the second week of May.

Market Action

BDCs were roughly flat over the week with an intra-sector double-digit range. Month-to-date the average BDC in our coverage is down slightly with CGBD leading to the downside.

The sector has been range-bound after clawing back close to half of its post-Liberation Day drop.

The median BDC valuation in our coverage is at a 12% discount which is about half-way between its recent low and its historic average level. The sector remains attractive for new allocations.

Market Themes

One theme of this earnings season is net unrealized depreciation, something we have seen in nearly all results so far in Q1. Unrealized depreciation refers to a net markdown of existing positions in the portfolio. It differs from a net realized loss in that the position remains on the books and could be written back up (or written down further) in the future.

Net unrealized depreciation is the primary driver of NAV falls in Q1. The median NAV drop is around 1% so far.

In a recent Weekly we discussed our expectation for widespread NAV falls in the sector and this is something we are seeing very clearly. The key factor behind this is the rise in public credit spreads - a chart of high-yield corporate credit spreads is shown below with the Q1-end marked. Over Q1, spreads have risen by around 0.65%.

Widespread net unrealized depreciation is not indicative of BDCs "losing money" or being "bad investments" just like falling equity prices are not necessarily indicative of corporates being bad investments. Sector prices were lower in Q1 nearly across the board and BDCs are not unusual here. The main reason for the weakness is a markdown of portfolio assets, more or less, corresponding to the move in public credit spreads. So far spreads are flat over Q2 so the base case scenario is for NAVs to remain roughly flat over Q2.

Market Commentary

Bain Capital Specialty Finance (BCSF) delivered a strong quarter with a 2.6% total NAV return - only below HTGC and FDUS so far in this early reporting season in our coverage. Very low level of interest expense of 4.8% - below that of ARCC - remains a key tailwind for the stock. A 14% discount is slightly wider than the sector median of 12% and average of 5%. The dividend remained the same with total dividend coverage of 110%. NII price yield is nearly 13%. On a valuation-adjusted basis the stock is one of the stronger ones in the sector.

Interestingly, BCSF fell nearly 4% mid-week despite these strong results. At current valuations, the Q1 total NAV return was actually the second best so far (of the 13 companies that have been processed).

The fact that NII beat the consensus suggests that NII wasn’t the culprit for the price fall. There is a tendency for a BDC to sell off on an NII miss, despite the fact that is not a great reason for the price to drop.

Maybe it’s possible to argue that the quality of the result was not as good as it looks because the net realized loss was offset by unrealized appreciation which looks odd in the context of what’s happening in the rest of the market with nearly every other BDC seeing unrealized depreciation due to wider credit spreads.

The fact that non-accruals rose on both at-cost and fair-value basis suggests some weakening in the portfolio which runs counter to net unrealized appreciation. It looks like new loans went on non-accrual as two previous non-accrual holdings were exited. Most likely there were a small number of individual write-ups that ran against the broader depreciation trend.

Elsewhere in the sector NMFC caught a bid, rising nearly 7% in the last 4 days of the week despite a tepid Q1 result. The 1.8% total NAV return is so far below the median reported (though above the average due to a couple of doozies which pushed the average well below the median). The relatively heavy equity allocation in the portfolio remains a risk for the stock. Despite a below-average valuation it does not screen as attractive on a valuation-adjusted basis (due to subpar performance).

Stance and Takeaways

This week we added CGBD as a new position. The stock is trading around its 1-year low while the average BDC is up around 10% from its respective low. CGBD is also 26% below its 1-year peak against a 20% for the average BDC.

The stock's valuation has now fallen to a double-digit discount against the sector average, after rising to a 5% premium late last year. At the time we highlighted the stock as being overly expensive.

We closed out our previous CGBD position well above current levels.

From a relative valuation perspective we sold our last holding when the stock's valuation was on parity with the sector. Now that it is at a double-digit discount against the sector (i.e. 18% CGBD discount vs. 5% average discount) we view it as attractive once again.

CGBD is trading at an 11.7% yield and an 18% discount to book.

data.title: Style Box Update: Growth Comes Roaring Back
data.description:
The above spreadsheet is an easy way for me to stay updated on the various style box returns based on the iShares style box ETFs which are listed on the spreadsheet.

As readers can see, large-cap growth led in 2023 and 2024, thanks to the two years of +25% returns for the S&P 500, but the value style held in much better and showed much better relative performance in 2023, before dramatically underperforming in 2024.

In the first three months of 2025 (through March 31), value outperformed nicely, but has now fallen back as growth has come roaring back, after tempered comments about tariffs following the sharp market drop around Liberation Day.

The vertical black-bordered boxes show style box returns for full years 2023 and 2024. The horizontal black-bordered box shows how badly SMID (small and mid-cap value) have performed in the last 10, 15 years.

The 10-year returns for mid-cap value and small-cap growth and small-cap value are just terrible. And yet, those are the styles that saw a lot of buying after the 1982 to 2000 bull market ended.

Summary/conclusion

These returns are updated just so I have a record of how the various styles are performing. None of this is advice or a recommendation. Large-cap growth is “lit” once again since April 7th, but the large-cap space is now largely very overbought.

I admire the discipline of small-cap value and mid-cap value investors, but I often wonder how their conversations with clients go, when the mainstream financial media is so focused on growth and tech, and those styles underperform.

If you gauge secular bull markets by length of time (as I do), this secular bull that started in March 2009 is getting a little elderly.

Keep an eye on what’s not doing well, even for long periods of time: when the bull market(s) end, as they inevitably do, the uncorrelated or non-correlated in the last bull market tend to shine.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All annual return data sourced above is from Morningstar. The above information may not be updated, and if it is updated, may not be done in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Artisan Partners Q1: Strong Start To 2025, Talented Team
data.description:
I rate Artisan Partners Asset Management Inc. (NYSE:APAM) a Buy, for income investors looking for a high-quality financial services stock. Artisan Partners is a global investment management firm that provides a broad range of high value-added investment strategies in growing asset classes to sophisticated clients around the world.

From the start of the year, there was a sharp drop in the stock price during the post-April tariff announcements and resulting volatility. The share price has recovered and is heading higher after Q1 results were announced.

The long-term performance records of their funds continually beat benchmarks. This is a result of the long-term tenure of many of their portfolio managers, who have worked for over 20 years with the firm, in many cases launched the fund. This performance is important to understand as “money chases performance.” Both individual and institutional clients are more likely to stay invested with the firm and add to their accounts after seeing the positive performance.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Pays quarterly dividend with a forward annual yield of 7.3%.
  • Governance is a core competency. The Board has significant experience and education. (ex. Worked at reputable companies such as Starwood Capital and educational institutions such as Harvard and Stanford)
  • Culture of hiring extraordinary talent and giving them the innovative tools to succeed.
  • Quarterly earnings in Q1 2025 beat estimates, showing growth in revenues, income, and AUM.
  • Total return shows an investor would double their money in less than 10 years.
  • 2025 is projected to be a positive year for financial firms with lower rates, deregulation, and growth of global wealth management.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Hold 2.97 (as of 5/16/25). Note the Valuation rating of A- and profitability of B+. The Quant rating is most likely affected by the sharp drop in stock price after the April tariff announcements.

Global Investment Management

Founded in 1994, and headquartered in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, Artisan Partners Asset Management Inc. is a publicly owned investment manager. It provides its services to pension and profit-sharing plans, trusts, endowments, foundations, charitable organizations, government entities, private funds, and non-U.S. funds, as well as mutual funds, non-U.S. funds and collective trusts. It manages separate client-focused equity and fixed income portfolios. The firm invests in the public equity and fixed income markets across the globe.

For the fixed income component of its portfolio the firm invests in non-investment-grade corporate bonds and secured and unsecured loans. It employs fundamental analysis to create its portfolios.

Artisan Partners Asset Management Inc. has additional offices in Atlanta, Georgia; New York City; San Francisco, California; Leawood, Kansas; and London, United Kingdom.

Q1 2025 Earnings Beat by $0.08

On April 29, the company declared Q1 2025 earnings, reporting the following financial highlights vs. prior year period:

  • Beat earnings estimates by $0.08.
  • AUM reached $162.4 billion, average AUM up +8% vs the year ago period.
  • Revenues of $277.1 million up +5%
  • Adjusted Operating Income of $89 million, up +9%
  • Adjusted Operating Margin of 32.1% up +120 bps.
  • Adjusted Net Income $67 million up +9%.

Overall, it was an excellent quarter showing growth in revenue and earnings driven by higher AUM. Keep in mind that this was for the period ending March 31 before the tariff shock on April 2nd. Still, the quarter proved the company, and its strategy are working, heading in the right direction. Given the lower rate environment with forthcoming deregulation, 2025 is projected to be a good year for financial companies.

Solid Performance but Lags the Sector

$10,000 invested in APAM over the past 10 years would have doubled the investment to $23,920. While the performance is good, it underperforms the Financial Sector index (XLF).

While it makes more sense to see this chart over a long period of time, the more recent charts would show a different story. In the past month or so, the stock is on the move, beating the sector index with various news announcements and a special dividend in Q4.

Valuation

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock an A- in valuation. A few metrics stood out to earn this grade. First, the price earnings ratio is 13.56 on a forward basis vs. 10.92 for the sector median, indicating that APAM is trading at a premium.

The dividend yield (TTM) of 6.72% is over double that of the sector median at 3.07%, indicating a better value for the money. Looking at the chart below, we can see that the quarterly dividend is paid out with the ebbs and flows of the cash collected.

In reviewing the past 10 years of dividend payouts, we can see in the chart below that the dividend payout amount is inconsistent, however the overall yield is very attractive.

In a comparison to peers, APAM has the second highest forward dividend yield of 7.3%, and a solid 5-year total return performance at 141%. Its price earnings of 15.2 indicates the stock is premium priced to most of the group.

Alliance Bernstein Holding L.P. (AB) stands out for having the highest dividend yield of the group. It has a solid 5-year total return of 192%. I recently reviewed the stock in my article “AllianceBernstein: High Distribution Yield with Growth Outlook.”

Victory Capital Holdings, Inc. (VCTR) has the best 5-year return of 422% based on growth of their client base and acquisitions. The company serves quite a different client.

Cohen & Steers, Inc. (CNS) has the highest price earnings ratio at 26.7, with the lowest 5-year total return. Given the high P/E and lowest dividend, CNS is trading at a premium based on its admiration of investors.

Federated Hermes Inc. (FHI) has the lowest price/earnings ratio at 9.8, with a solid 5-year total return.

Outlook

APAM has strong momentum going into 2025. The increased AUM will drive management fees and the actual fund performances with retain existing clients and attract new ones.

From a macroeconomic perspective, 2025 is shaping up to be an attractive year for financial companies. Scott Bessent, Secretary of the Treasury, has talked about policies that involve a focus on keeping taxes low, balancing trade, and deregulation, which are all favorable for investment. Within international markets, the U.S. is still seen as having the reserve currency, with the best investment environment on the globe (“U.S. exceptionalism”).

APAM will announce Q2 earnings on July 21, 2025.

Risks To Rating

The largest risk I see to APAM is direct competition. There are many competitors in this space making it challenging to stay on top. They appear to have a niche focused on top talent that have long tenures with the firm. This feature of the company certainly has its appeal.

Bottom Line

I rate Artisan Partners a Buy for income investors interested in a high-quality financial services stock. APAM has offices globally, serving both individual and institutional clients with a broad set of products and services. Their funds have outperformed the indexes and are managed by long-term portfolio managers with strong experience and education. All of this makes the company more attractive to existing clients and appealing to new clients.

After a drop in price due to the tariff shocks, shares of APAM have been climbing back. The current dividend yield is 7.3%, paid out on a quarterly basis.

Q1 2025 results were strong, showing a growth in revenue, income, and AUM. Projecting out the rest of the year, APAM has the potential to have a record year. This is helped by the current administration’s policy of lower rates, lower taxes, and deregulation.

Finally, the company has a core competency in their governance, in my view. The board is a group of exceptionally talented individuals with relevant experience and education at the top of the field. This will serve APAM well as the company develops winning strategies and attracts talent in the future.

data.title: Under Armour: Rating Downgrade To Sell As Valuation Upgrade Is Not Justified
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to Under Armour, Inc. (NYSE:UAA) previously, with my key thesis being that Under Armour stock is at a crossroads that could go both ways depending on whether growth can turn positive. Unfortunately, 4Q25 results showed no real progress. Revenue continues to decline, DTC demand remains weak without promotions, and footwear is still a drag. The tariff risks and a lack of traction in the North America reset also make it hard to believe that a turnaround will happen anytime soon. Despite that, valuation has gone up. As such, I am downgrading to a sell rating.

Earnings results update

UAA 4Q25 results, in my view, did not show any positive developments to shift the narrative. This is still a business under pressure. Revenue fell 11% y/y to ~$1.2 billion (down 10% on an FX-neutral basis). Geographically, North America was down 11% y/y, Asia-Pacific was down 27%, EMEA fell 2%, and Latin America fell 10%. By channels, wholesale fell 10% y/y, and DTC was worse, down 15%, with e-commerce down 27%. Gross margin was the one bright spot, expanding 165 bps to 46.7%, but was more than offset by higher SG&A, which increased 7% y/y to $586 million. That resulted in an adj. EBIT loss of $36 million and an adjusted EPS loss of $0.08. All in, this quarter reinforces my view that the US is still deep in the woods.

Revenue Growth Shows No Signs of Recovery

The biggest issue for UAA right now, in my opinion, is the continued revenue decline. Revenue fell 11% y/y in 4Q25, and guidance calls for another 4–5% decline in 1Q26. This doesn’t exactly instill confidence that the worst is over. Here is how I think of the UAA situation: If it cannot stabilize revenue despite efforts to reposition itself, it says a lot about how consumers are engaging (or not engaging) with its product. Specifically, as I noted before, UAA has stepped up product innovation and marketing, but despite that, revenue is still down. This makes the path forward to growth recovery look much more difficult.

This weakness is especially clear in North America, with revenue here down 11% y/y in 4Q25, and there is no sign of a near-term turnaround. Management's reason for the weakness was due to the soft spring/summer wholesale order books, which is puzzling since management noted that the 18-month reset for the region started in May 2024. And yet, there is little tangible progress. This implication is huge because if UAA cannot regain footing in its core market (this is where UAA has the most brand equity), it paints a very weak outlook for its international market.

The other concern is the continued weakness in DTC, especially e-commerce. Management explained the decline as a result of pulling back on promotions to support the brand’s premium positioning. This makes sense in theory, but the scale of the decline is significant, and what this tells me is that the full-price repositioning is not working—i.e., consumers are not pivoting back to paying full price for UAA products. In other words, without promotions, demand is not as great. The implication here is that if UAA cannot grow DTC without relying on heavy promotions, it heavily limits its ability to improve the brand narrative (say, to become more premium), which limits margin expansion potential, and also to reduce dependence on the wholesale channel. So far, this strategy to cut back on promotion is not working, and I don’t think the market will give credit to this strategy until we see a huge positive inflection in growth.

As for the footwear segment, it has also remained a major drag. Similar to the DTC segment, weakness here was framed as proactive portfolio management, but in my view, this weakness is a red flag. My belief is that a strong footwear brand is essential for any performance brand (like Nike, Adidas, and, in this case, UAA). The idea is that footwear drives brand relevance and consumer interest. Currently, UAA is not delivering on this front. And with management warning that footwear could face a little more pressure in 1Q26, I believe it is telling that the worst is far from over. If UAA cannot rebuild brand equity and momentum here, I think it will have a very hard time capturing consumers' interest ahead.

Tariff Threats Add Another Layer of Complexity

The current tariff situation comes at a horrible time for UAA. Management itself has acknowledged on the earnings call that changes in trade policy are expected to have a “significant impact,” and they’re still figuring out how to respond. The way I see it, this adds a major layer of uncertainty to UAA’s equity story. With ~30% of sourcing tied to Vietnam and another ~15% from Indonesia, UAA has sizable exposure. Mitigation strategies like shifting sourcing or negotiating with manufacturing partners might help, but they take time and may not fully offset the impact. Given that demand is already weak, these tariffs could make sustaining margins a lot more difficult. And if pricing has to go up, it could push more consumers away (remember that full-price demand is already weak). This is a meaningful new overhang on the stock sentiment. Not only could it pressure demand and margins, but it also significantly increases the turnaround complexity.

Valuation

For a business that has not shown any signs of demand turning around and many visible headwinds, I was surprised that its valuation has inflected upwards, now back to its 3-year average of 18x forward P/E. I am puzzled about this move. My take is that the demand outlook is still extremely weak, and it could get a lot worse if the US macroeconomy turns for the worse. The market appears to be pricing in a near-term recovery, which I struggle to convince myself will happen. As a comparison, Puma trades at 15x forward PE with mid-to-high-single-digit revenue growth. UAA, on the other hand, trades at 18x forward P/E with low-single-digit revenue growth.

I believe there is more downside than upside at this current valuation, and if UAA were to continue reporting 1 or 2 more quarters of poor results, the market is likely to de-rate multiples back to where it traded earlier, at low-teens multiples.

Investment Risk

If new products like the Halo collection work and the new marketing strategy can connect with consumers, UAA’s brand momentum could recover quicker than expected. That could lead to better traffic and full-price sell-through. Additionally, a faster-than-expected recovery in North America or any easing of the current tariff uncertainty could also shift the outlook meaningfully.

Conclusion

I give a sell rating for UAA. Revenue continues to decline across regions and channels, and key initiatives like the North America reset showed little traction. The weakness in the footwear segment and new tariff risks add more layers of uncertainty. Despite all this, UAA trades at 18x forward P/E—well above what, I think, is justified given the weak fundamentals. Unless there’s a clear inflection in demand or a major upside surprise, I believe there’s more downside risk from here as expectations reset.
